    22→      - Contingency Fees: 10–30% of funds raised, with the firm managing all campaign accounting to secure payment.

    34→    1.  Stipends: Initial payments to cover startup work.

2026.03.06 <-->Paradigm - March 06
VIEW RECORDING: https://fathom.video/share/ymL_7iWxNb5nqyxuEkzccGpW2ajvjn9z
Meeting Purpose

Finalize the business plan for a new political consulting firm.

Key Takeaways

  - Business Model: A for-profit LLC consulting for US political campaigns, using a "volunteer-to-paid" pipeline. Free "Campaign in a Box" guides will attract clients, with revenue from contingency fees (10–30% of funds raised) and flat fees for self-funded campaigns.
  - Equitable Pay: The firm will target a $175k annual salary for founders, a figure chosen as the minimum needed to thrive (e.g., cover student loans) in a high-cost area. A 10:1 wage parity ratio (CEO to lowest-paid employee) was proposed to ensure fairness.
  - Legal & Financial Strategy: The firm will use a pass-through LLC/S-Corp structure to leverage tax benefits and protect personal assets. Contingency fee contracts will be iron-tight, with the firm managing all campaign accounting to secure payment.
  - Team Roles: Core competencies were defined: Kristine (tech/ops), Maude (social media strategy), Alex (data science), Matt (legal), and Brittany/Kevin (campaign/financial strategy). A US-based social media counterpart is needed for on-the-ground content creation.

Topics

Business Model & Niche

  - Structure: For-profit LLC consulting exclusively for US political campaigns and parties.
      - Rationale: Avoids the saturated, low-margin nonprofit fundraising market and prevents legal/retaliatory risks associated with mixing political and nonprofit activities.
  - Revenue Model: A hybrid approach combining flat fees and contingency fees.
      - Flat Fees: For self-funded campaigns, estimated at ~$10k/month.
      - Contingency Fees: 10–30% of funds raised, with the firm managing all campaign accounting to secure payment.
  - Go-to-Market: A "volunteer-to-paid" pipeline using free resources to attract clients.
      - Free "Campaign in a Box": A resource hub with how-to guides on campaign setup, fundraising, strategy, and social media.
      - Free Strategy Calls: 1-hour consultations to build trust and demonstrate value, serving as the primary sales funnel.
      - Initial Client Pipeline: Includes 45+ candidates and the California National Party.

Financials & Pay Structure

  - Founder Salary Target: $175,000/year.
      - Rationale: A baseline for thriving, not just surviving, in a high-cost area.
  - Wage Parity: A 10:1 ratio (CEO to lowest-paid employee) was proposed to ensure equitable pay.
  - Phased Compensation:
    1.  Stipends: Initial payments to cover startup work.
    2.  1099 Contractors: Paid based on hours/commission.
    3.  W-2 Employees: Full-time staff, requiring ~33% added to salary for taxes and benefits.
  - Prioritization: Payments will be prioritized by financial need and work completed.
      - Reimbursements: Startup costs will be reimbursed, with priority given to those who need it back immediately.
      - Maude: Voluntarily deferred payment to prioritize team members with greater financial need.

Legal & Risk Management

  - Entity Structure: LLC owned by an S-Corp.
      - Rationale: Provides pass-through tax benefits and an extra layer of separation to protect personal assets from legal/political targeting.
  - Non-Compete Clauses: Ruled out.
      - Rationale: The FTC's new rule, effective August 20, 2024, invalidates new non-competes.
      - Alternative: A non-disclosure agreement (NDA) with a high penalty for misuse of proprietary information.
  - Regulatory Compliance: Strict adherence to federal regulations (FinCEN) is critical.
      - Rationale: The firm will have a "target on its back" due to its anti-corruption mission.
      - Safeguard: A clear separation of entities and roles (e.g., no principal officer overlap) is required to avoid legal issues.

Team Roles & Competencies

  - Kristine: Tech infrastructure, system integration (CRM, accounting), secure portals, and financial backstop.
  - Maude: Social media strategy, content production, algorithm manipulation, and modern media training.
  - Alex: Data science (Python, R, SQL) for policy advising and party identification.
  - Matt: Legal drafting (contracts, SOWs) and managing external counsel.
  - Brittany: Campaign management, policy advising, and media training.
  - Kevin: Financial strategy, accounting oversight, and legal structure.
  - Need: A US-based social media counterpart for on-the-ground content creation and travel.

Next Steps

  - All:
      - Add expertise-specific content to the "Campaign in a Box" section of the shared doc.
      - Define individual roles and core competencies in the shared doc.
      - Complete the "When to Meet" poll to find a recurring meeting time.
  - Brittany:
      - Schedule the next meeting based on poll results.
  - Kristine:
      - Create a Signal chat for the legal/finance team (Kristine, Kevin, Matt).
      - Share the Airtable database of startup programs and software discounts.
  - Matt:
      - Research subscription-based legal services (e.g., LegalShield) for contract review.
  - Alex:
      - Identify third parties that are close to ballot-access thresholds and align with the firm's anti-corruption mission.
Action Items
  - Add social media tips to Campaign in a Box - WATCH (5 secs): https://fathom.video/share/ymL_7iWxNb5nqyxuEkzccGpW2ajvjn9z?timestamp=482.087406
  - Add initials to assigned items in agenda doc - WATCH (5 secs): https://fathom.video/share/ymL_7iWxNb5nqyxuEkzccGpW2ajvjn9z?timestamp=533.087406
  - Draft modern media training program w/ Brittany & Kevin - WATCH (5 secs): https://fathom.video/share/ymL_7iWxNb5nqyxuEkzccGpW2ajvjn9z?timestamp=1444.087406
  - Draft secure tech training (Proton, portals, data) - WATCH (5 secs): https://fathom.video/share/ymL_7iWxNb5nqyxuEkzccGpW2ajvjn9z?timestamp=2558.087405999999
  - Draft NDA/non-disclosure terms for contractor agreements - WATCH (5 secs): https://fathom.video/share/ymL_7iWxNb5nqyxuEkzccGpW2ajvjn9z?timestamp=2700.087405999999
  - Collect hours/immediate needs from team via Signal - WATCH (5 secs): https://fathom.video/share/ymL_7iWxNb5nqyxuEkzccGpW2ajvjn9z?timestamp=3309.087405999999

     4→Equitable Structure: The firm will be an employee-owned co-op with proportional pay (e.g., a 1:5 ratio between lowest and highest earners) and a $175k/year salary goal for founders.
     5→Immediate Action: A business plan draft is due Friday, March 6, covering legal structure, services, and financial strategy. The next meeting is Monday to review.

     7→Business Model & Niche
     8→Decision: The firm will be a for-profit LLC
     9→Focus: Strictly political consulting for candidates and parties who are anti-corruption.

    11→Go-to-Market: The "volunteer-to-paid" pipeline leverages Brittany's network of ~45 candidates to build a portfolio and generate initial revenue. Free resources for candidates ALWAYS. Some free advising. Want the team? Contingency fee basis + flat fees and leftover funds. Flat fee for tech set up. Flat fee for treasurer?

    49→Compensation Philosophy: An employee-owned co-op with an equitable pay structure.

    51→Goal: A $175k/year salary for founders, requiring ~$100k/month in revenue.
    52→Initial Phase: The team will start as 1099 contractors. Prioritize immediate needs first.

   111→build out regional teams that can handle the day-to-day
   112→these teams will eventually need to build out more local teams to be on the ground for campaigns
   113→Obviously, biggest markets first (I.e. if we're doing lots of work in San Fransisco, a team needs to be there, if we're doing a lot of work in Topeka, KS, a team needs to be there. "Biggest markets" does not refer to most populous or popular)
   114→Automation, automation, automation
   115→Execs are out of the weeds

   118→traveling (semi)regularly to meet with regional teams and assess needs/team build
   119→Still remote-first (assuming we're remote-first, right?) 
   120→LEGAL
   121→VERY separate from Unrig & Other Ecosystem Buckets
   122→Each can do "side projects" but needs to be a strong contract detailing do's and don't's
   123→Some crossover between LLC and NPO is likely necessary, but need to be very clear on when we are doing work for which org, guardrails, etc
   124→dot our t's and cross our i's because we'll have targets on our backs
   125→should create an S-corp to have ownership of the LLC and keep our names out of public record
   126→also helps with taxes
   127→again, let's use the loopholes to our advantage
   128→Could also incorporate in a state that does not require personally identifiable info to be put into record
   129→regardless, we'll still need to comply with FinCEN regulations

Key Takeaways
Niche Defined: The firm will be a for-profit LLC, strictly consulting for US political campaigns and parties.
Revenue Model: A "volunteer-to-paid" pipeline will convert pro-bono work into contingency-based contracts (e.g., % of funds raised), a necessary model for candidates with no upfront budget.
Equitable Structure: The firm will be an employee-owned co-op with proportional pay (e.g., a 1:5 ratio between lowest and highest earners) and a $175k/year salary goal for founders.
Immediate Action: A business plan draft is due Friday, March 6, covering legal structure, services, and financial strategy. The next meeting is Monday to review.
Topics
Business Model & Niche
Decision: The firm will be a for-profit LLC
Focus: Strictly political consulting for candidates and parties who are anti-corruption.
Rationale: The NPO fundraising market is saturated; the political market offers higher revenue potential (e.g., $5k–$15k/month per local campaign; national campaigns could be as much as $60k/month).
Go-to-Market: The "volunteer-to-paid" pipeline leverages Brittany's network of ~45 candidates to build a portfolio and generate initial revenue. Free resources for candidates ALWAYS. Some free advising. Want the team? Contingency fee basis + flat fees and leftover funds. Flat fee for tech set up. Flat fee for treasurer?

Campaign Manager Contract: If we invest in training them, they must stick with us. Don't want managers taking all of our training and becoming competitors. Incentive should be considered in addition to contract.

AI Policy: Use AI for trend analysis and efficiency, but all public-facing content must be human-created to maintain credibility.
Company Structure & Compensation
Legal Entity: An LLC, potentially owned by an S-Corp for tax benefits.
Rationale: Provides liability protection and a clear path for revenue.
Compensation Philosophy: An employee-owned co-op with an equitable pay structure.
Principle: Proportional pay (e.g., 1:5 ratio) to prevent large salary gaps.
Goal: A $175k/year salary for founders, requiring ~$100k/month in revenue.
Initial Phase: The team will start as 1099 contractors. Prioritize immediate needs first.

content.content:
1→Starting a US Political "Campaign in a Box" Consulting Firm
     2→Executive Summary
     3→US political spending is now a multi‑tens‑of‑billions‑per‑cycle market, with federal campaign and party committees alone spending over $23 billion in the 2023–2024 cycle, plus billions more in independent expenditures and dark‑money activity. This environment supports a growing ecosystem of political consulting firms, including full‑service "one‑stop shops" like Axiom Strategies that reported around $196 million in net revenue in the 2022 cycle and projected $250 million for 2024.[1][2][3][4]
     4→A specialized consulting firm offering a standardized "campaign in a box" for down‑ballot candidates can occupy a clear niche: professionalizing under‑resourced campaigns with brand, fundraising, digital, and infrastructure support that is normally only available to higher‑tier races. The model should lean on a mix of modest retainers and productized packages rather than heavy reliance on percentage‑of‑funds structures, which are controversial in fundraising ethics and can create perverse incentives, especially for smaller campaigns.[5][6][7][8]
     5→This report outlines the core problem, target market, service stack, suggested pricing structures, high‑level revenue modeling, and key strategic and legal considerations for launching such a firm.
     6→Core Problem Definition
     7→Most local and state‑level candidates lack access to professionalized campaign infrastructure even as expectations for digital sophistication, constant content, and disciplined fundraising have escalated due to national‑level norms. Many first‑time or working‑class candidates in particular struggle to leave existing jobs, master compliance, hire staff, and build the tech stack to compete, despite recent FEC rule changes that make it somewhat easier for federal candidates to draw salaries from their campaign funds.[9][10][11][7]
     8→This creates a gap between well‑funded, consultant‑backed campaigns and under‑resourced challengers who may have strong alignment with their communities but cannot execute at the same professional level in message discipline, fundraising, or voter contact.
     9→Market Size and Dynamics
    10→Overall Political Spending
    11→Federal candidates, party committees, and PACs raised and spent enormous sums in the 2023–2024 cycle: presidential candidates raised about $2 billion and spent $1.8 billion, congressional candidates raised roughly $3.8 billion and spent $3.7 billion, party committees raised $2.7 billion and spent $2.6 billion, and PACs raised approximately $15.7 billion and spent $15.5 billion. Independent expenditures associated with federal races reached roughly $4.4 billion, and political ad spend for the 2024 presidential race alone is estimated at over $12 billion.[1][9]
    12→USAFacts and OpenSecrets data indicate total federal election spending in 2024 exceeded prior records and is likely above $15.9 billion, with elections becoming steadily more expensive each cycle. This does not include state and local races, ballot initiatives, or issue advocacy, all of which contribute additional demand for consulting and campaign services.[12][2]
    13→Size of the Addressable Down‑Ballot Market
    14→There are 7,386 state legislative seats nationwide, with thousands of these up for election in each even‑year cycle and additional seats in odd‑year races. In 2026, regular elections will be held for 88 of the 99 state legislative chambers, again putting several thousand seats on the ballot.[13][8]
    15→On top of this, hundreds of large US cities and thousands of smaller municipalities hold city, county, and school‑board races in both on‑cycle and off‑cycle elections, ensuring a continual stream of local contests rather than a single federal peak every two years. This means a "campaign in a box" provider can build a year‑round pipeline by mixing federal‑cycle races with local and special elections.[14][15]
    16→Consulting Fee Benchmarks
    17→Fundraising and nonprofit consultants commonly charge flat project fees, hourly rates, or monthly retainers, with hourly rates often in the range of 76–150 dollars and retainers ranging from 2,500 to well above 25,000 dollars per month for full‑service engagements. Political consulting for higher‑level races can be significantly more expensive, with some full‑service firms charging 50,000 to 250,000 dollars per month for state and national races, though these figures are for large‑scale operations and are not realistic for most local campaigns.[16][17][18][5]
    18→These benchmarks suggest that for local and down‑ballot state races, a consulting firm can realistically charge a few thousand dollars per month for a productized package, while maintaining acceptable margins if delivery is standardized and highly automated.
    19→Target Segment and Positioning
    20→The most natural initial target segment is under‑resourced local and state‑legislative campaigns that are ideologically aligned with the firm’s values but lack in‑house expertise and cannot afford top‑tier national consulting firms. These candidates typically need help with basic infrastructure, fundraising systems, and coherent, credible messaging more than exotic analytics or national‑level media buys.[18][8]
    21→Positioning as a "full stack" but standardized solution for campaigns under a certain fundraising threshold (for example, those projecting 50,000 to 500,000 dollars in total cycle fundraising) creates a clear niche between volunteer‑only efforts and bespoke, high‑cost firms. Over time, the firm can add separate offerings for parties, caucuses, and PACs that want a scalable way to support slates of local candidates.[18]
    22→Proposed "Campaign in a Box" Service Stack
    23→1. Branding and Platform Definition
    24→Core deliverables could include candidate narrative development, values and issues hierarchy, and design of a visual identity package (logos, color palette, typography) tailored to local norms and ballot context. A standardized messaging framework and content calendar template can help inexperienced candidates stay on narrative across mail, digital, and live appearances.[3]
    25→2. Fundraising Engine
    26→A bundled fundraising system should include donor universe mapping, email and SMS infrastructure setup, call time workflows, basic prospect research templates, and recurring small‑dollar programs (e.g., monthly sustainers). For many down‑ballot campaigns, simply professionalizing call time and small‑dollar funnels can double or triple realistic fundraising capacity compared with purely volunteer‑run efforts.[6][5]
    27→Consulting ethics literature highlights that while percentage‑based fundraising fees are attractive, they are controversial because they can encourage hard‑sell tactics that damage donor relationships and create misaligned incentives. For a for‑profit political consulting firm, a hybrid of modest retainers plus capped performance bonuses based on milestone achievements (e.g., reaching specific monthly fundraising targets) is generally more sustainable and reputationally safer than pure contingency.[5][6]
    28→3. Marketing and Digital
    29→Digital marketing components can include website creation on a standard template, list‑building lead magnets, email and SMS content, and organic social strategy with tailored posting cadences for platforms like Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, and X. For smaller campaigns, a heavy focus on organic reach and community engagement is often more cost‑effective than large paid ad budgets.[19][9]
    30→Given the user’s AI policy concept, internal use of AI can focus on monitoring trends, generating first‑draft analytical insights, and helping prioritize content opportunities, while public‑facing copy, scripts, and design are finalized by human staff to maintain authenticity and guard against AI‑generated misfires.[20]
    31→4. Infrastructure and Operations
    32→A core differentiator is bundling basic but robust infrastructure: compliance‑friendly contribution processing, email/SMS tools, a CRM, call time and door‑knocking workflows, volunteer sign‑up flows, and simple reporting dashboards. By packaging this into a repeatable, semi‑automated tech stack, the firm can keep marginal costs low and rely on standardized onboarding.[7]
    33→Operations support can also include simple financial systems (budget templates, cash‑flow planning), basic HR guidance for campaign hires, and coaching on FEC or state‑level rules at a high level, with recommendations to seek specialist counsel for complex compliance questions.[21][22]
    34→5. Training and Coaching
    35→Training should cover candidate media coaching, debate prep, field program basics, and campaign manager development, likely delivered via a mix of live sessions and reusable curriculum materials. This creates leverage: one well‑designed training track can be rolled out to multiple campaigns concurrently, improving unit economics.[3]
    36→Pricing and Business Model
    37→Recommended Pricing Structure
    38→Given benchmarks and risk considerations, a hybrid structure is advisable:
    39→·       Upfront setup fee: A fixed fee (e.g., 5,000–15,000 dollars depending on race size) covering branding, tech stack setup, initial strategic plan, and core training. This fee should be payable in installments to reduce friction while still covering real initial labor.[17][5]
    40→·       Monthly retainer: A recurring fee tiered by service level (for example, 2,000–5,000 dollars per month for local races, higher for state‑legislative slates) covering ongoing strategic support, content, and operations.[16][5]
    41→·       Performance bonuses: Clearly defined bonuses tied to milestones (e.g., hitting 20,000, 50,000, 100,000 dollars in cumulative fundraising; meeting voter‑contact targets in field programs), but not as a simple uncapped percentage of funds raised.
    42→Pure percentage‑of‑funds models, while legal in the political context, mirror fee structures that fundraising ethics bodies discourage for nonprofits because they can undermine donor trust and encourage short‑termism. A capped or milestone‑based bonus structure keeps incentives aligned without overexposing the firm to client underperformance.[6][5]
    43→Rough Revenue Modeling
    44→Assume an initial focus on 10–15 local and state‑legislative campaigns in the first full cycle:
    45→·       If the average setup fee is 8,000 dollars and the average retainer is 3,000 dollars per month for 8 months of active campaigning, each client generates approximately 32,000 dollars per cycle (8,000 plus 24,000).[17][5]
    46→·       At 12 clients, this yields about 384,000 dollars per cycle in core revenue, before any performance bonuses or add‑on services.
    47→As capacity, reputation, and automation improve, scaling to 30–40 clients across staggered races and adding higher‑ticket party or PAC engagements can move annual revenue into the low‑ to mid‑seven‑figure range, similar in structure (though not in scale) to one‑stop political firms such as Axiom Strategies.[3][18]
    48→Wage Structure and Hiring Strategy
    49→Early‑Stage Reality vs. Long‑Term Wage Parity
    50→Industry compensation for fundraising and campaign consultants is typically framed in terms of hourly rates between 75 and 250 dollars or retainers of several thousand dollars per month, with benefits varying by firm size. A wage‑parity philosophy (for example, holding maximum‑to‑minimum pay ratios around 10:1) is a principled stance that contrasts with corporate norms where ratios can exceed 200:1, but it must be reconciled with startup volatility.[5][17]
    51→Given the revenue model above, fully loaded executive‑level salaries at 15,000 dollars per month (including benefits and payroll taxes) imply annualized costs of about 180,000 dollars per executive‑level staff member. For a firm with, say, 500,000 to 1,000,000 dollars in early annual revenue, this quickly consumes more than the intended 50 percent of expenses allocated to salaries unless headcount growth is tightly controlled and non‑executive compensation is modest.
    52→This argues for a phased approach:
    53→·       Start with a small founding team taking modest guaranteed cash plus upside via profit‑sharing or equity‑like arrangements.
    54→·       Use stipends and 1099 contracts for part‑time contributors, tied to clear deliverables and billable hours, until client volume stabilizes.
    55→·       Transition to full salaries with parity policies once the firm consistently clears a target level of monthly recurring revenue (MRR) and has multi‑cycle visibility.
    56→Using Contract and Stipend Phases
    57→The proposed approach of beginning with stipends for those actively working toward the firm’s goals, then layering in 1099 contractors, and finally moving to salaried roles is aligned with cash‑constrained startup practice in both consulting and political fields. Tying a small percentage of fundraising income to retroactive payouts once a campaign crosses a threshold (such as 20,000 dollars per month raised) is one way to reward early labor without overcommitting to fixed payroll.[23][17]
    58→However, to manage risk, performance‑linked payouts should be contractually precise, capped, and backed by clear collection mechanisms so that campaigns cannot easily withhold payments once milestones are met.
    59→Incorporation, Location, and Legal Considerations
    60→Entity Choice and Jurisdiction
    61→A limited liability company (LLC) with pass‑through taxation is a common structure for small consulting firms; some founders use an S‑corporation or holding company for ownership and tax optimization, including around self‑employment taxes. Incorporating in a state that offers favorable tax treatment and does not require disclosure of personal owner information on public records is possible, but federal rules such as the Corporate Transparency Act and related Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) beneficial‑ownership reporting regimes still apply.[24][25][22]
    62→For a firm operating nationally, there will also be questions of registering as a foreign entity in states where it has significant operations or employees. Separating a for‑profit consulting LLC from any associated non‑profit entity is crucial to avoid commingling of funds or activities that could jeopardize tax‑exempt status.
    63→Campaign‑Finance and Compliance Issues
    64→The Federal Election Commission (FEC) sets contribution limits for individuals, PACs, and parties to federal candidates, which indirectly shape campaign budgets and the amount available to pay consultants; for 2025–2026, the per‑election limit for individual contributions to a candidate has risen to 3,500 dollars, or 7,000 dollars per cycle. FEC guidance for party committees and candidates also establishes compliance requirements around fundraising, independent expenditures, and reporting that any consulting firm must understand at a practical level.[26][22][21]
    65→Recent FEC rules now allow federal candidates to pay themselves salaries from campaign funds earlier in the campaign and at up to 50 percent of the minimum House salary or their average income over five years, which may slightly improve the pool of viable clients who can run without personal wealth. However, this also increases scrutiny of how campaigns allocate funds, including to consultants, and reinforces the need for clear arms‑length contracts and invoicing.[10][11]
    66→Key Assumptions and Risks
    67→Several core assumptions underlie the "campaign in a box" model:
    68→·       That there is a sufficiently large pool of under‑resourced candidates who can still afford modest retainers and whose values align with the firm.
    69→·       That campaigns will accept standardized packages rather than fully bespoke work, enabling significant automation and margin.
    70→·       That a hybrid retainer‑plus‑bonus structure will prove as attractive to candidates as pure contingency models offered by some fundraisers.
    71→Risks include:
    72→·       Cyclicality and lumpiness: Even with off‑cycle city and special elections, revenue will be uneven across years without deliberate smoothing via multi‑year party or PAC contracts.[15][14]
    73→·       Collections risk: Under‑funded campaigns can go dark or simply fail to pay, making contractual protections and staged delivery‑for‑payment milestones critical.
    74→·       Regulatory and reputational risk: Close proximity to campaign finance means high scrutiny; mixing a for‑profit consulting LLC with any related nonprofit must be handled with strict firewalls and legal advice.[22]
    75→Strategic Roadmap (0–24+ Months)
    76→0–6 Months: Foundation and First Pilots
    77→·       Form the LLC and any S‑corp or holding structure after consulting counsel on state‑of‑incorporation and FinCEN obligations.[22]
    78→·       Build the standardized tech stack (donation processing, CRM, email/SMS, website template, reporting) and training curriculum.
    79→·       Draft master service agreements, statements of work, and clear fee/bonus language, including non‑payment safeguards.
    80→·       Identify and sign 2–4 pilot campaigns for the next available cycle, ideally in districts or cities where founders have strong networks.
    81→6–12 Months: Refinement and Niche Definition
    82→·       Run the first campaigns through the full "campaign in a box" offering and measure key outcomes: funds raised, voter contact, win/loss rate.
    83→·       Refine pricing and scope based on actual delivery cost and client feedback, avoiding over‑customization.
    84→·       Begin outreach to party committees or caucuses interested in standardized support for slates of similar candidates.
    85→12–24 Months: From Projects to a Portfolio
    86→·       Shift revenue mix so that at least 60–75 percent comes from repeatable, contract‑based work (retainers, slate or party contracts), not ad‑hoc or pro bono engagements.
    87→·       Decide on a primary niche based on early success—e.g., specific states, types of office, or ideological lane.
    88→·       Begin hiring regional or local contractors who can handle on‑the‑ground execution while central leadership focuses on sales, product, and relationships.
    89→24+ Months: Scale and Delegation
    90→·       Build regional pods in the geographies producing the most work, with local teams managing day‑to‑day while executives handle firm strategy and key relationships.[18]
    91→·       Further automate onboarding, reporting, and content workflows so that leadership can step out of daily execution and focus on growth, quality control, and long‑term partnerships.
    92→·       Explore complementary revenue streams such as training cohorts, digital products for candidates, or white‑labeled infrastructure for aligned organizations.
    93→Areas for Further Expert Input
    94→Several aspects of this plan warrant detailed consultation with specialized counsel and experienced operatives:
    95→·       Detailed state‑level campaign finance rules, especially for cities and school boards, which can materially affect pricing, permissible relationships, and billing models.
    96→·       The optimal mix of for‑profit and non‑profit structures if the firm intends to operate both as a mission‑driven infrastructure provider and a commercial consultant.
    97→·       Long‑term talent strategy, including when to shift from 1099 contractors to W‑2 employees, how to implement wage‑parity policies while remaining competitive, and which benefits to prioritize at each growth stage.
    98→
    99→

Starting a US Political "Campaign in a Box" Consulting Firm
Executive Summary
US political spending is now a multi‑tens‑of‑billions‑per‑cycle market, with federal campaign and party committees alone spending over $23 billion in the 2023–2024 cycle, plus billions more in independent expenditures and dark‑money activity. This environment supports a growing ecosystem of political consulting firms, including full‑service "one‑stop shops" like Axiom Strategies that reported around $196 million in net revenue in the 2022 cycle and projected $250 million for 2024.[1][2][3][4]
A specialized consulting firm offering a standardized "campaign in a box" for down‑ballot candidates can occupy a clear niche: professionalizing under‑resourced campaigns with brand, fundraising, digital, and infrastructure support that is normally only available to higher‑tier races. The model should lean on a mix of modest retainers and productized packages rather than heavy reliance on percentage‑of‑funds structures, which are controversial in fundraising ethics and can create perverse incentives, especially for smaller campaigns.[5][6][7][8]
This report outlines the core problem, target market, service stack, suggested pricing structures, high‑level revenue modeling, and key strategic and legal considerations for launching such a firm.
Core Problem Definition
Most local and state‑level candidates lack access to professionalized campaign infrastructure even as expectations for digital sophistication, constant content, and disciplined fundraising have escalated due to national‑level norms. Many first‑time or working‑class candidates in particular struggle to leave existing jobs, master compliance, hire staff, and build the tech stack to compete, despite recent FEC rule changes that make it somewhat easier for federal candidates to draw salaries from their campaign funds.[9][10][11][7]
This creates a gap between well‑funded, consultant‑backed campaigns and under‑resourced challengers who may have strong alignment with their communities but cannot execute at the same professional level in message discipline, fundraising, or voter contact.
Market Size and Dynamics
Overall Political Spending
Federal candidates, party committees, and PACs raised and spent enormous sums in the 2023–2024 cycle: presidential candidates raised about $2 billion and spent $1.8 billion, congressional candidates raised roughly $3.8 billion and spent $3.7 billion, party committees raised $2.7 billion and spent $2.6 billion, and PACs raised approximately $15.7 billion and spent $15.5 billion. Independent expenditures associated with federal races reached roughly $4.4 billion, and political ad spend for the 2024 presidential race alone is estimated at over $12 billion.[1][9]
USAFacts and OpenSecrets data indicate total federal election spending in 2024 exceeded prior records and is likely above $15.9 billion, with elections becoming steadily more expensive each cycle. This does not include state and local races, ballot initiatives, or issue advocacy, all of which contribute additional demand for consulting and campaign services.[12][2]
Size of the Addressable Down‑Ballot Market
There are 7,386 state legislative seats nationwide, with thousands of these up for election in each even‑year cycle and additional seats in odd‑year races. In 2026, regular elections will be held for 88 of the 99 state legislative chambers, again putting several thousand seats on the ballot.[13][8]
On top of this, hundreds of large US cities and thousands of smaller municipalities hold city, county, and school‑board races in both on‑cycle and off‑cycle elections, ensuring a continual stream of local contests rather than a single federal peak every two years. This means a "campaign in a box" provider can build a year‑round pipeline by mixing federal‑cycle races with local and special elections.[14][15]
Consulting Fee Benchmarks
Fundraising and nonprofit consultants commonly charge flat project fees, hourly rates, or monthly retainers, with hourly rates often in the range of 76–150 dollars and retainers ranging from 2,500 to well above 25,000 dollars per month for full‑service engagements. Political consulting for higher‑level races can be significantly more expensive, with some full‑service firms charging 50,000 to 250,000 dollars per month for state and national races, though these figures are for large‑scale operations and are not realistic for most local campaigns.[16][17][18][5]
These benchmarks suggest that for local and down‑ballot state races, a consulting firm can realistically charge a few thousand dollars per month for a productized package, while maintaining acceptable margins if delivery is standardized and highly automated.
Target Segment and Positioning
The most natural initial target segment is under‑resourced local and state‑legislative campaigns that are ideologically aligned with the firm’s values but lack in‑house expertise and cannot afford top‑tier national consulting firms. These candidates typically need help with basic infrastructure, fundraising systems, and coherent, credible messaging more than exotic analytics or national‑level media buys.[18][8]
Positioning as a "full stack" but standardized solution for campaigns under a certain fundraising threshold (for example, those projecting 50,000 to 500,000 dollars in total cycle fundraising) creates a clear niche between volunteer‑only efforts and bespoke, high‑cost firms. Over time, the firm can add separate offerings for parties, caucuses, and PACs that want a scalable way to support slates of local candidates.[18]
Proposed "Campaign in a Box" Service Stack
1. Branding and Platform Definition
Core deliverables could include candidate narrative development, values and issues hierarchy, and design of a visual identity package (logos, color palette, typography) tailored to local norms and ballot context. A standardized messaging framework and content calendar template can help inexperienced candidates stay on narrative across mail, digital, and live appearances.[3]
2. Fundraising Engine
A bundled fundraising system should include donor universe mapping, email and SMS infrastructure setup, call time workflows, basic prospect research templates, and recurring small‑dollar programs (e.g., monthly sustainers). For many down‑ballot campaigns, simply professionalizing call time and small‑dollar funnels can double or triple realistic fundraising capacity compared with purely volunteer‑run efforts.[6][5]
Consulting ethics literature highlights that while percentage‑based fundraising fees are attractive, they are controversial because they can encourage hard‑sell tactics that damage donor relationships and create misaligned incentives. For a for‑profit political consulting firm, a hybrid of modest retainers plus capped performance bonuses based on milestone achievements (e.g., reaching specific monthly fundraising targets) is generally more sustainable and reputationally safer than pure contingency.[5][6]
3. Marketing and Digital
Digital marketing components can include website creation on a standard template, list‑building lead magnets, email and SMS content, and organic social strategy with tailored posting cadences for platforms like Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, and X. For smaller campaigns, a heavy focus on organic reach and community engagement is often more cost‑effective than large paid ad budgets.[19][9]
Given the user’s AI policy concept, internal use of AI can focus on monitoring trends, generating first‑draft analytical insights, and helping prioritize content opportunities, while public‑facing copy, scripts, and design are finalized by human staff to maintain authenticity and guard against AI‑generated misfires.[20]
4. Infrastructure and Operations
A core differentiator is bundling basic but robust infrastructure: compliance‑friendly contribution processing, email/SMS tools, a CRM, call time and door‑knocking workflows, volunteer sign‑up flows, and simple reporting dashboards. By packaging this into a repeatable, semi‑automated tech stack, the firm can keep marginal costs low and rely on standardized onboarding.[7]
Operations support can also include simple financial systems (budget templates, cash‑flow planning), basic HR guidance for campaign hires, and coaching on FEC or state‑level rules at a high level, with recommendations to seek specialist counsel for complex compliance questions.[21][22]
5. Training and Coaching
Training should cover candidate media coaching, debate prep, field program basics, and campaign manager development, likely delivered via a mix of live sessions and reusable curriculum materials. This creates leverage: one well‑designed training track can be rolled out to multiple campaigns concurrently, improving unit economics.[3]
Pricing and Business Model
Recommended Pricing Structure
Given benchmarks and risk considerations, a hybrid structure is advisable:
·       Upfront setup fee: A fixed fee (e.g., 5,000–15,000 dollars depending on race size) covering branding, tech stack setup, initial strategic plan, and core training. This fee should be payable in installments to reduce friction while still covering real initial labor.[17][5]
·       Monthly retainer: A recurring fee tiered by service level (for example, 2,000–5,000 dollars per month for local races, higher for state‑legislative slates) covering ongoing strategic support, content, and operations.[16][5]
·       Performance bonuses: Clearly defined bonuses tied to milestones (e.g., hitting 20,000, 50,000, 100,000 dollars in cumulative fundraising; meeting voter‑contact targets in field programs), but not as a simple uncapped percentage of funds raised.
Pure percentage‑of‑funds models, while legal in the political context, mirror fee structures that fundraising ethics bodies discourage for nonprofits because they can undermine donor trust and encourage short‑termism. A capped or milestone‑based bonus structure keeps incentives aligned without overexposing the firm to client underperformance.[6][5]
Rough Revenue Modeling
Assume an initial focus on 10–15 local and state‑legislative campaigns in the first full cycle:
·       If the average setup fee is 8,000 dollars and the average retainer is 3,000 dollars per month for 8 months of active campaigning, each client generates approximately 32,000 dollars per cycle (8,000 plus 24,000).[17][5]
·       At 12 clients, this yields about 384,000 dollars per cycle in core revenue, before any performance bonuses or add‑on services.
As capacity, reputation, and automation improve, scaling to 30–40 clients across staggered races and adding higher‑ticket party or PAC engagements can move annual revenue into the low‑ to mid‑seven‑figure range, similar in structure (though not in scale) to one‑stop political firms such as Axiom Strategies.[3][18]
Wage Structure and Hiring Strategy
Early‑Stage Reality vs. Long‑Term Wage Parity
Industry compensation for fundraising and campaign consultants is typically framed in terms of hourly rates between 75 and 250 dollars or retainers of several thousand dollars per month, with benefits varying by firm size. A wage‑parity philosophy (for example, holding maximum‑to‑minimum pay ratios around 10:1) is a principled stance that contrasts with corporate norms where ratios can exceed 200:1, but it must be reconciled with startup volatility.[5][17]
Given the revenue model above, fully loaded executive‑level salaries at 15,000 dollars per month (including benefits and payroll taxes) imply annualized costs of about 180,000 dollars per executive‑level staff member. For a firm with, say, 500,000 to 1,000,000 dollars in early annual revenue, this quickly consumes more than the intended 50 percent of expenses allocated to salaries unless headcount growth is tightly controlled and non‑executive compensation is modest.
This argues for a phased approach:
·       Start with a small founding team taking modest guaranteed cash plus upside via profit‑sharing or equity‑like arrangements.
·       Use stipends and 1099 contracts for part‑time contributors, tied to clear deliverables and billable hours, until client volume stabilizes.
·       Transition to full salaries with parity policies once the firm consistently clears a target level of monthly recurring revenue (MRR) and has multi‑cycle visibility.
Using Contract and Stipend Phases
The proposed approach of beginning with stipends for those actively working toward the firm’s goals, then layering in 1099 contractors, and finally moving to salaried roles is aligned with cash‑constrained startup practice in both consulting and political fields. Tying a small percentage of fundraising income to retroactive payouts once a campaign crosses a threshold (such as 20,000 dollars per month raised) is one way to reward early labor without overcommitting to fixed payroll.[23][17]
However, to manage risk, performance‑linked payouts should be contractually precise, capped, and backed by clear collection mechanisms so that campaigns cannot easily withhold payments once milestones are met.
Incorporation, Location, and Legal Considerations
Entity Choice and Jurisdiction
A limited liability company (LLC) with pass‑through taxation is a common structure for small consulting firms; some founders use an S‑corporation or holding company for ownership and tax optimization, including around self‑employment taxes. Incorporating in a state that offers favorable tax treatment and does not require disclosure of personal owner information on public records is possible, but federal rules such as the Corporate Transparency Act and related Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) beneficial‑ownership reporting regimes still apply.[24][25][22]
For a firm operating nationally, there will also be questions of registering as a foreign entity in states where it has significant operations or employees. Separating a for‑profit consulting LLC from any associated non‑profit entity is crucial to avoid commingling of funds or activities that could jeopardize tax‑exempt status.
Campaign‑Finance and Compliance Issues
The Federal Election Commission (FEC) sets contribution limits for individuals, PACs, and parties to federal candidates, which indirectly shape campaign budgets and the amount available to pay consultants; for 2025–2026, the per‑election limit for individual contributions to a candidate has risen to 3,500 dollars, or 7,000 dollars per cycle. FEC guidance for party committees and candidates also establishes compliance requirements around fundraising, independent expenditures, and reporting that any consulting firm must understand at a practical level.[26][22][21]
Recent FEC rules now allow federal candidates to pay themselves salaries from campaign funds earlier in the campaign and at up to 50 percent of the minimum House salary or their average income over five years, which may slightly improve the pool of viable clients who can run without personal wealth. However, this also increases scrutiny of how campaigns allocate funds, including to consultants, and reinforces the need for clear arms‑length contracts and invoicing.[10][11]
Key Assumptions and Risks
Several core assumptions underlie the "campaign in a box" model:
·       That there is a sufficiently large pool of under‑resourced candidates who can still afford modest retainers and whose values align with the firm.
·       That campaigns will accept standardized packages rather than fully bespoke work, enabling significant automation and margin.
·       That a hybrid retainer‑plus‑bonus structure will prove as attractive to candidates as pure contingency models offered by some fundraisers.
Risks include:
·       Cyclicality and lumpiness: Even with off‑cycle city and special elections, revenue will be uneven across years without deliberate smoothing via multi‑year party or PAC contracts.[15][14]
·       Collections risk: Under‑funded campaigns can go dark or simply fail to pay, making contractual protections and staged delivery‑for‑payment milestones critical.
·       Regulatory and reputational risk: Close proximity to campaign finance means high scrutiny; mixing a for‑profit consulting LLC with any related nonprofit must be handled with strict firewalls and legal advice.[22]
Strategic Roadmap (0–24+ Months)
0–6 Months: Foundation and First Pilots
·       Form the LLC and any S‑corp or holding structure after consulting counsel on state‑of‑incorporation and FinCEN obligations.[22]
·       Build the standardized tech stack (donation processing, CRM, email/SMS, website template, reporting) and training curriculum.
·       Draft master service agreements, statements of work, and clear fee/bonus language, including non‑payment safeguards.
·       Identify and sign 2–4 pilot campaigns for the next available cycle, ideally in districts or cities where founders have strong networks.
6–12 Months: Refinement and Niche Definition
·       Run the first campaigns through the full "campaign in a box" offering and measure key outcomes: funds raised, voter contact, win/loss rate.
·       Refine pricing and scope based on actual delivery cost and client feedback, avoiding over‑customization.
·       Begin outreach to party committees or caucuses interested in standardized support for slates of similar candidates.
12–24 Months: From Projects to a Portfolio
·       Shift revenue mix so that at least 60–75 percent comes from repeatable, contract‑based work (retainers, slate or party contracts), not ad‑hoc or pro bono engagements.
·       Decide on a primary niche based on early success—e.g., specific states, types of office, or ideological lane.
·       Begin hiring regional or local contractors who can handle on‑the‑ground execution while central leadership focuses on sales, product, and relationships.
24+ Months: Scale and Delegation
·       Build regional pods in the geographies producing the most work, with local teams managing day‑to‑day while executives handle firm strategy and key relationships.[18]
·       Further automate onboarding, reporting, and content workflows so that leadership can step out of daily execution and focus on growth, quality control, and long‑term partnerships.
·       Explore complementary revenue streams such as training cohorts, digital products for candidates, or white‑labeled infrastructure for aligned organizations.
Areas for Further Expert Input
Several aspects of this plan warrant detailed consultation with specialized counsel and experienced operatives:
·       Detailed state‑level campaign finance rules, especially for cities and school boards, which can materially affect pricing, permissible relationships, and billing models.
·       The optimal mix of for‑profit and non‑profit structures if the firm intends to operate both as a mission‑driven infrastructure provider and a commercial consultant.
·       Long‑term talent strategy, including when to shift from 1099 contractors to W‑2 employees, how to implement wage‑parity policies while remaining competitive, and which benefits to prioritize at each growth stage.

  1. Home

  3. Blog
  4. /
  5. Rebuilding Resilience
Research & Frameworks February 10, 2026

Rebuilding Resilience: Frameworks, Metrics, and Research for Communities and Individuals

A research compilation covering conceptual overviews, neighborhood-level frameworks, quantitative indices, and psychological resilience scales—tools for understanding, measuring, and strengthening resilience at every level.

Resilience is used in several overlapping ways: as the capacity to withstand shocks, the dynamic process of adapting over time, and the outcomes that signal successful recovery or transformation. It helps to explicitly distinguish personal psychological resilience from community or neighborhood resilience, and then borrow concrete frameworks and metrics from each.[1][2][3][4]

Big-Picture Conceptual Overviews

What Do We Mean by “Community Resilience”? A Systematic Literature Review of How It Is Defined and Measured
Patel, S.S. et al. (2017) — PLOS Currents Disasters
Synthesizes dozens of definitions and frameworks for community resilience, identifies common elements (local knowledge, networks, communication, governance, health), and discusses implications for practice and measurement.[2]

Resilience as Pathway Diversity: Linking Systems, Individual and Societal Resilience
Lade, S.J. et al. (2019) — arXiv
Proposes resilience as “pathway diversity” in social–ecological systems, giving language to talk about resilience as multiple possible futures rather than simply “bouncing back.”[4]

Community Resilience: A Multidisciplinary Exploration for Inclusive Frameworks
(2024) — ScienceDirect
Provides an updated multidisciplinary review, clustering community resilience work into key themes (infrastructure, social capital, governance, equity), useful to structure essay sections and program design.[5]

Community and Neighborhood Resilience Frameworks

EnRiCH Community Resilience Framework for High-Risk Populations
O’Sullivan, T.L. et al. (2014) — PLOS Currents Disasters
Puts “adaptive capacity” at the center, surrounded by four strategic areas (leadership, asset/resource management, awareness/communication, connectedness/engagement) driven by empowerment, innovation, and collaboration; helpful for describing levers neighborhoods can actually pull.[6]

IFRC Framework for Community Resilience
International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies (2014) — PDF
Practice-oriented framework showing how to support communities to self-mobilize, strengthen local organizations, and link everyday well-being work to disaster resilience.[7]

Creating the Conditions for Community Resilience
DOAJ (open access)
Develops a conceptual framework summarizing common requirements for community resilience (e.g., social capital, inclusive governance, local capabilities), giving language for “upstream” neighborhood work beyond emergency planning.[8]

Community Resilience to Pandemics: An Assessment Framework Integrating Social, Built and Natural Environments
(2022) — Europe PMC
Offers a concrete, indicator-based framework for assessing pandemic resilience at community scale, illustrating how to operationalize “resilience” in terms of environment, health systems, and social factors.[9]

Community Resilience to Disasters: Measuring and Improving Community Resilience
De Iuliis, M. et al. (2022) — arXiv
Uses the PEOPLES framework (Population, Environmental, Organized governmental services, Physical infrastructure, Lifestyle, Economic, Social–cultural) with fuzzy logic to quantify resilience, useful as an example of making resilience computable.[10]

Quantitative Indices and Metrics

A Value-Focused Thinking Approach to Measure Community Sustainable Resilience
Suresh, R. et al. (2024) — arXiv
Builds an objectives hierarchy and indicators across social, economic, infrastructure, and environmental dimensions; reviews existing indices like the Community Disaster Resilience Index and Resilience Capacity Index, which can be referenced as examples of composite “resilience scores.”[11][3]

Deep Learning-Driven Community Resilience Rating Based on Social–Ecological–Technological Systems
(Resili-Net, 2023) — arXiv
Develops a machine-learning rating system for urban resilience using features like infrastructure robustness, facility access, and social conditions, showing how newer work uses big data to map resilience across neighborhoods.[12]

Development and Validation of a Community Resilience Scale for Youth
Silva, S.M. et al. (2022) — PLOS ONE
Provides a psychometrically validated survey to assess youths’ perception of community resilience; a good example of “bottom-up” measurement grounded in lived experience.[13]

Community Resilience to Pandemics (above) and Social Resources and Community Resilience in the Wake of Disasters
Cagney, K.A. et al. (2016) — PLOS ONE
Introduces the Resilience Activation Framework, focusing on how access to social resources (formal and informal support) activates resilience capacities; useful for making “social infrastructure” measurable.[14][15]

Personal Psychological Resilience: Frameworks and Scales

Resilience Resources, Coping Strategies and Positive Adaptation
Fullerton, D.J. et al. (2021) — PLOS ONE
Integrates several theoretical models (e.g., resilience as dynamic process) and tests a path model linking resilience resources, coping strategies, and outcomes; helpful for writing about mechanisms rather than traits.[1]

Conceptualizing and Measuring Psychological Resilience: What Can We Learn from Existing Models?
(review article) — ScienceDirect
Argues for defining psychological resilience in terms of dynamic responses to stressors and reviews measurement approaches, including outcome-based and process-based metrics.[16]

A Conceptual Framework for the Neurobiological Study of Resilience
Kalisch, R. et al. (2015) — bioRxiv/PubMed
Proposes how to quantify resilience as relatively preserved mental health under stress, with implications for how to think about “resilience as outcome” in longitudinal terms.[17]

The ART of Resilience: A Theoretical Bridge Across Approaches
Frontiers in Psychology (2025) — Frontiers
Introduces the ART framework (Acknowledgment of resources, Reframing stressors, Tailoring responses) as a practical, process-oriented model that can be translated into everyday practices for individuals and groups.[18]

Community vs Personal Resilience: A Quick Comparison

Aspect Community / Neighborhood Resilience Personal Resilience
Core idea Collective capacity to withstand, adapt, recover, and transform.[2][6] Individual’s dynamic ability to maintain or regain functioning under stress.[1][16]
Typical frameworks EnRiCH; IFRC; Resilience Activation; PEOPLES; pandemic resilience models.[6][7][15][10][9] ART framework; resilience resources models; neurobiological resilience.[18][1][17]
Common metrics Indices across social, economic, infrastructure, environmental, governance dimensions.[11][3][9] Scales like CD-RISC variants, coping patterns, mental health outcomes over time.[1][16]
Levers for change Strengthening local networks, leadership, services, built environment, equity.[6][8][7] Building coping skills, reframing, social support use, meaning-making.[1][18]

These pieces should give you: (1) language for different meanings of “resilience”; (2) specific neighborhood-level frameworks you can narrate through stories or local examples; and (3) validated metrics you can reference or adapt into informal “resilience audits” for readers.

Sources

  1. Fullerton, D.J. et al. — Resilience Resources, Coping Strategies and Positive Adaptation (PLOS ONE, 2021) ↑
  2. Patel, S.S. et al. — What Do We Mean by “Community Resilience”? (PLOS Currents Disasters, 2017) ↑
  3. Suresh, R. et al. — A Value-Focused Thinking Approach to Measure Community Sustainable Resilience (arXiv, 2024) ↑
  4. Lade, S.J. et al. — Resilience as Pathway Diversity (arXiv, 2019) ↑
  5. Community Resilience: A Multidisciplinary Exploration for Inclusive Frameworks (ScienceDirect, 2024) ↑
  6. O’Sullivan, T.L. et al. — EnRiCH Community Resilience Framework (PLOS Currents Disasters, 2014) ↑
  7. IFRC Framework for Community Resilience (2014) ↑
  8. Creating the Conditions for Community Resilience (DOAJ) ↑
  9. Community Resilience to Pandemics: An Assessment Framework (Europe PMC, 2022) ↑
  10. De Iuliis, M. et al. — Community Resilience to Disasters: Measuring and Improving (arXiv, 2022) ↑
  11. Suresh, R. et al. — Value-Focused Thinking Approach (arXiv PDF, 2024) ↑
  12. Resili-Net — Deep Learning-Driven Community Resilience Rating (arXiv, 2023) ↑
  13. Silva, S.M. et al. — Community Resilience Scale for Youth (PLOS ONE, 2022) ↑
  14. Cagney, K.A. et al. — Social Resources and Community Resilience (PLOS ONE, 2016) ↑
  15. Resilience Activation Framework (PMC, 2014) ↑
  16. Conceptualizing and Measuring Psychological Resilience (ScienceDirect) ↑
  17. Kalisch, R. et al. — Conceptual Framework for Neurobiological Study of Resilience (2015) ↑
  18. The ART of Resilience: A Theoretical Bridge Across Approaches (Frontiers in Psychology, 2025) ↑
  19. PubMed 39715248
  20. Semantic Scholar — Resilience Study (PDF)
  21. arXiv 2411.08015
  22. Semantic Scholar — Resilience Research (PDF)
  23. arXiv 2511.12798v1
  24. Semantic Scholar — Community Resilience (PDF)
  25. arXiv 2306.08795
  26. arXiv 1803.07650

  29. GWU Center for Community Resilience
  30. ASPR — Community Resilience (HHS)

  32. Nature — Resilience Research (2025)

76→ Technology & Privacy 77→ March 6, 2026 78→
79→

Your AI Chats Can Be Used Against You: What You Need to Know in 2026

80→

A federal court has ruled that your conversations with AI chatbots have no legal privilege. Whether you use ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or DeepSeek, here is what is actually happening with your data — and what you can do about it.

81→
82→ 89→ 90→
91→ 92→
93→

The Key Finding

94→

In February 2026, a federal judge ruled that conversations between a person and an AI chatbot are not protected by attorney-client privilege and can be compelled as evidence in court. This applies to every major AI platform — ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and others.[1][2]

95→
96→ 97→
98→ 99→

1. The Courts Have Spoken: AI Chats Are Evidence

100→ 101→

If you have ever used an AI chatbot to talk through a legal question, process difficult emotions, draft sensitive documents, or discuss private matters, you should know: those conversations can be obtained by courts, law enforcement, and opposing attorneys just like your text messages or emails.

102→ 103→

Here are the key rulings from 2025–2026:

104→ 105→

US v. Heppner (February 2026)

106→

Judge Jed Rakoff of the Southern District of New York ruled that conversations between a criminal defendant and Anthropic's Claude chatbot are not protected by attorney-client privilege or work product doctrine. The reasoning: an AI is not a lawyer, and "discussion of legal issues between two non-attorneys is not protected." The defendant had no reasonable expectation of confidentiality.[1][2]

107→ 108→

NYT v. OpenAI: 20 Million Chat Logs (January 2026)

109→

In the ongoing New York Times copyright lawsuit, a judge ordered OpenAI to turn over its entire 20-million-log sample of ChatGPT conversations. OpenAI tried to provide only cherry-picked logs; the court rejected that. Only Enterprise, Education, and Zero Data Retention customers are excluded from the preservation order.[3]

110→

If you have ever used ChatGPT's consumer interface, your conversations may be in that dataset.

111→ 112→

FBI Seizure of a CEO's AI Chats (2026)

113→

The FBI seized a CEO's computer and sought access to his AI chat history. The court left only a narrow, untested exception: if an attorney specifically directed the client to conduct AI research as part of attorney work product.[4]

114→ 115→

Criminal Prosecutions and Family Court

116→

In 2025, three criminal cases cited ChatGPT conversations as evidence — involving child exploitation, arson, and vandalism. Multiple divorce and custody cases in 2024–2026 have used ChatGPT and Claude chat histories as evidence. California courts are now implementing AI document review for family law cases.[5][6]

117→ 118→
119→

Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, has publicly warned that people treating ChatGPT like a therapist or lawyer should understand those conversations can be subpoenaed.[7]

120→
121→ 122→

A 2025 survey found that half of AI users are unaware their chats can be compelled as evidence.[8]

123→ 124→
125→ 126→

2. What Each AI Company Does with Your Data

127→ 128→

Not all AI services handle your data the same way. Here is what you need to know about the major providers as of March 2026.

129→ 130→

OpenAI (ChatGPT, GPT-5.4)

131→ 132→ 133→ 134→ 135→ 136→ 137→ 138→ 139→ 140→
SettingWhat Happens to Your Data
Chat history ON (default)Retained indefinitely; used for model training
Chat history OFFRetained 30 days for abuse monitoring, then deleted; not used for training
API (developer)30-day retention for safety; not used for training by default
Enterprise/TeamNever used for training; Zero Data Retention available for qualifying customers
141→ 142→
143→

Critical risks: OpenAI operates a monitoring system that scans all ChatGPT conversations for potentially harmful content, escalating concerning conversations to human reviewers who can report to law enforcement.[9] Additionally, the NYT litigation hold requires preservation of all consumer data regardless of your privacy settings.[3]

144→
145→ 146→

New development (Feb–Mar 2026): OpenAI has contracted to provide AI for classified Pentagon networks. While OpenAI claims three "red lines" (no mass domestic surveillance, no autonomous weapons), the Electronic Frontier Foundation has criticized the contractual language as insufficient to prevent misuse.[10][11]

147→ 148→

In the first half of 2025 alone, OpenAI received 119 government requests for user account information and 26 requests for chat content.[9]

149→ 150→

Anthropic (Claude)

151→ 152→ 153→ 154→ 155→ 156→ 157→ 158→ 159→ 160→
SettingWhat Happens to Your Data
"Help improve Claude" ONRetained 5 years (de-identified); used for training
"Help improve Claude" OFFRetained 30 days, then deleted; not used for training
Trust & safety flaggedUp to 2 years (inputs/outputs); classification scores up to 7 years
API / Business / EnterpriseAnthropic acts as data processor; never used for training
161→ 162→

Notable: Anthropic refused a Pentagon contract for weapons and surveillance use, and was subsequently blacklisted by the current administration. This is a meaningful values signal for those who care about how their AI provider relates to military and surveillance uses.[12]

163→ 164→

However, Anthropic is still US-based (San Francisco) and subject to the same legal framework as OpenAI — including National Security Letters, FISA courts, and subpoenas. The US v. Heppner ruling specifically addressed Claude conversations.[1]

165→ 166→

Google (Gemini)

167→ 168→ 169→ 170→ 171→ 172→ 173→ 174→ 175→ 176→
SettingWhat Happens to Your Data
Gemini app (default)Stored up to 30 days for debugging and abuse detection
Gemini Apps Activity OFFNot saved, not used for training; metadata still logged
Vertex AI (business)Zero Data Retention available per API call; EU data residency configurable
Google WorkspaceCustomer data not used for AI training without permission
177→ 178→

Google offers the best EU data residency options among major US providers, including configurable data regions on Vertex AI (Belgium, Frankfurt, etc.).[13]

179→ 180→
181→

Watch out: A class action lawsuit (Oct 2025) alleges Google activated Gemini AI features across Gmail, Chat, and Meet without user consent, enabling AI to read private communications.[14] A separate federal jury awarded $425.7 million to ~98 million Google users misled about data collection opt-outs.[15]

182→
183→ 184→

DeepSeek

185→ 186→
187→

All data stored in the People's Republic of China. No specific retention periods disclosed. Third-party analysis found the DeepSeek app can capture login information and share it with China Mobile, a state-owned entity.[16]

188→
189→ 190→

DeepSeek has been banned in Italy, South Korea, Texas, New York, Tennessee, and Virginia (on government devices), with pending federal legislation (HR 1121) to ban it on all US government devices. Belgium, France, Ireland, and Germany have active regulatory inquiries.[17][18]

191→ 192→

Under China's National Security Law, Chinese companies are required to provide data to the government on request — with no judicial oversight.

193→ 194→
195→ 196→

3. The Privacy Scorecard: Comparing Your Options

197→ 198→

Here is how the major AI providers compare on privacy risk as of March 2026:

199→ 200→ 201→ 202→ 203→ 204→ 205→ 206→ 207→ 208→ 209→ 210→ 211→
ProviderUS JurisdictionData RetentionTraining Opt-OutOverall Risk
ChatGPT (consumer)Yes (CA)Indefinite (30d if history off)AvailableHIGHEST
Claude (consumer)Yes (CA)5yr if opted in; 30d if notAvailableHIGHEST
Gemini (consumer)Yes (CA)30 daysAvailableHIGH
DeepSeek (API)ChinaUnspecified; stored in PRCUnclearHIGHEST
Self-hosted (Llama 4, Mistral 3, etc.)Your choiceYou controlN/ALOWEST
212→ 213→
214→ 215→

4. Regulations Are Catching Up — But Slowly

216→ 217→

GDPR Enforcement Is Real

218→
    219→
  • Italy fined OpenAI €15 million (December 2024)[19]
    • 220→
  • Italy fined Replika €5 million (May 2025)[20]
    • 221→
  • Italy banned DeepSeek entirely (January 2025)[17]
    • 222→
  • Over $3.5 billion in AI governance fines and settlements paid by Big Tech in 2025 alone[21]
    • 223→
224→ 225→

New US State Laws (Effective January 1, 2026)

226→ 227→ 228→ 229→ 230→ 231→ 232→ 233→ 234→ 235→
StateLawWhat It Does
CaliforniaSB 53Risk management disclosure for frontier models (companies with revenue >$500M)
CaliforniaAB 2013Training data transparency requirements
TexasRAIGAProhibits AI systems that incite harm, deepfakes, CSAM
ColoradoComprehensive AI ActComing June 2026
IllinoisEmployer AI DisclosureAI disclosure requirements for employers
236→ 237→

EU AI Act Timeline

238→
    239→
  • Already in force: Banned practices (manipulative AI, mass surveillance) since February 2025
    • 240→
  • August 2026: High-risk AI requirements become enforceable (employment, credit, education, law enforcement)
    • 241→
  • Penalties: Up to €35 million or 7% of global turnover[22]
    • 242→
243→ 244→
245→ 246→

5. How to Protect Yourself

247→ 248→

You cannot make AI conversations legally privileged — no court has recognized that protection. But you can significantly reduce your risk.

249→ 250→

Immediate Steps (Do These Today)

251→
    252→
  1. Turn off training data sharing on every AI service you use: 253→
      254→
    • ChatGPT: Settings → Data Controls → "Improve the model for everyone" → OFF
      • 255→
    • Claude: Settings → Privacy Settings → "Help improve Claude" → OFF
      • 256→
    • Gemini: Gemini Apps Activity → Toggle OFF "Save Gemini Apps Activity"
      • 257→
    258→
    2. 259→
  2. Never put these things in an AI chat: 260→
      261→
    • Real names of people in vulnerable situations
      • 262→
    • Immigration status information
      • 263→
    • Legal strategy (there is no attorney-client privilege)
      • 264→
    • Medical records or health details
      • 265→
    • Financial account information
      • 266→
    267→
    3. 268→
  3. Delete your AI chat history regularly — but know that deletion does not affect data already preserved under litigation holds or law enforcement requests.
    4. 269→
  4. Do not use DeepSeek's API if you or anyone in your conversations could be at risk from the Chinese government.
    5. 270→
271→ 272→

Stronger Protections (If Privacy Is Critical)

273→
    274→
  1. Use API access instead of consumer interfaces. When you use ChatGPT.com or Claude.ai directly, your data is subject to consumer privacy policies with long retention periods and litigation holds. API access with business agreements (Data Processing Agreements) provides stronger protections — your data is not used for training, and the provider acts as a data processor, not a data owner.
    2. 275→
  2. Consider EU-hosted AI services. Google's Vertex AI offers configurable EU data residency, meaning your data can stay in European data centers subject to GDPR protections. This is the strongest GDPR posture among the major US providers.[13]
    3. 276→
  3. Explore self-hosted open-source models. For maximum privacy, you can run AI models on your own hardware where no data ever leaves your control: 277→
      278→
    • Llama 4 Scout (Meta, open-weight) — fits on a single GPU, 10 million token context window
      • 279→
    • Mistral Large 3 (Apache 2.0 license) — fully open-source, 256K context
      • 280→
    • DeepSeek V3.1 (MIT License) — self-hosted version has zero external data transmission
      • 281→
    282→
    4. 283→
284→ 285→
286→

The safest option: Self-hosted, open-source AI models on servers in privacy-friendly jurisdictions (like Iceland or Switzerland). No company can be subpoenaed because no company has your data.

287→
288→ 289→
290→ 291→

6. Special Concerns for Vulnerable Communities

292→ 293→

If you work with or belong to communities that face heightened surveillance risk — immigrants, activists, journalists, domestic violence survivors, LGBTQ+ individuals in hostile jurisdictions — the stakes are even higher.

294→ 295→
    296→
  • National Security Letters (NSLs) can be served directly to US-based AI companies with a gag order that prevents the company from telling you. All major AI providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) are US-based and subject to this.[23]
    • 297→
  • FISA Section 702 is due to expire in April 2026 — its renewal could expand or limit surveillance powers.
    • 298→
  • The US CLOUD Act allows US authorities to compel data from US providers even if stored abroad.
    • 299→
  • OpenAI's content monitoring system scans all conversations and can escalate to law enforcement without your knowledge.[9]
    • 300→

Google (Gemini)

SettingWhat Happens to Your Data
Gemini app (default)Stored up to 30 days for debugging and abuse detection
Gemini Apps Activity OFFNot saved, not used for training; metadata still logged
Vertex AI (business)Zero Data Retention available per API call; EU data residency configurable

Here is how the major AI providers compare on privacy risk as of March 2026:

ProviderUS JurisdictionData RetentionTraining Opt-OutOverall Risk

  • Italy fined OpenAI €15 million (December 2024)[19]
  • Italy fined Replika €5 million (May 2025)[20]

StateLawWhat It Does

  • August 2026: High-risk AI requirements become enforceable (employment, credit, education, law enforcement)
  • Penalties: Up to €35 million or 7% of global turnover[22]

5. How to Protect Yourself

You cannot make AI conversations legally privileged — no court has recognized that protection. But you can significantly reduce your risk.

Immediate Steps (Do These Today)

  1. Turn off training data sharing on every AI service you use:
    • ChatGPT: Settings → Data Controls → "Improve the model for everyone" → OFF
    • Claude: Settings → Privacy Settings → "Help improve Claude" → OFF
    • Gemini: Gemini Apps Activity → Toggle OFF "Save Gemini Apps Activity"
  2. Never put these things in an AI chat:
    • Real names of people in vulnerable situations
    • Immigration status information
    • Legal strategy (there is no attorney-client privilege)
    • Medical records or health details
    • Financial account information
  3. Delete your AI chat history regularly — but know that deletion does not affect data already preserved under litigation holds or law enforcement requests.
  4. Do not use DeepSeek's API if you or anyone in your conversations could be at risk from the Chinese government.

Stronger Protections (If Privacy Is Critical)

  1. Use API access instead of consumer interfaces. When you use ChatGPT.com or Claude.ai directly, your data is subject to consumer privacy policies with long retention periods and litigation holds. API access with business agreements (Data Processing Agreements) provides stronger protections — your data is not used for training, and the provider acts as a data processor, not a data owner.
  2. Consider EU-hosted AI services. Google's Vertex AI offers configurable EU data residency, meaning your data can stay in European data centers subject to GDPR protections. This is the strongest GDPR posture among the major US providers.[13]
  3. Explore self-hosted open-source models. For maximum privacy, you can run AI models on your own hardware where no data ever leaves your control:
    • Llama 4 Scout (Meta, open-weight) — fits on a single GPU, 10 million token context window
    • Mistral Large 3 (Apache 2.0 license) — fully open-source, 256K context
    • DeepSeek V3.1 (MIT License) — self-hosted version has zero external data transmission

The safest option: Self-hosted, open-source AI models on servers in privacy-friendly jurisdictions (like Iceland or Switzerland). No company can be subpoenaed because no company has your data.

6. Special Concerns for Vulnerable Communities

If you work with or belong to communities that face heightened surveillance risk — immigrants, activists, journalists, domestic violence survivors, LGBTQ+ individuals in hostile jurisdictions — the stakes are even higher.

  • National Security Letters (NSLs) can be served directly to US-based AI companies with a gag order that prevents the company from telling you. All major AI providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) are US-based and subject to this.[23]
  • FISA Section 702 is due to expire in April 2026 — its renewal could expand or limit surveillance powers.
  • The US CLOUD Act allows US authorities to compel data from US providers even if stored abroad.
  • OpenAI's content monitoring system scans all conversations and can escalate to law enforcement without your knowledge.[9]

Situational Awareness & Risk Assessment

Early Warning Systems:

  • Monitor verified local news and police scanner apps (Broadcastify) for real-time updates.
  • Use social media monitoring tools like TweetDeck to follow local hashtags and official accounts, but verify information before acting.[1]

Technology & Privacy February 11, 2026

Data Privacy & Sovereignty Best Practices (Feb 2026)

The most effective way to prevent your data being weaponized for surveillance is to reduce what is collected, limit who can access it, and keep it within jurisdictions and systems you trust.

1. Understand How Your Data Is Weaponized

Modern "surveillance capitalism" monetizes and weaponizes behavioral data – location, browsing, biometrics, intimate communications – to profile, manipulate, or target people.[1][2][3][4]

Key risks:

  • Profiling and targeting: Detailed behavioral profiles fuel political micro‑targeting, discriminatory advertising, and credit/insurance decisions.[5][1]
  • Government access and data trafficking: States can compel or secretly access corporate data, often across borders (for example the U.S. CLOUD Act lets U.S. authorities demand data from U.S. providers even if stored abroad).[6][4]
  • Sensitive data abuse: Gender identity, health status, location, or reproductive‑health data can be used to target marginalized groups or prosecute them, as documented in recent analysis of U.S. gender‑politics data uses.[4][5]
The goal of your strategy is to shrink your "attack surface": less data collected, fewer intermediaries, stronger technical controls, and better jurisdictional choices.

2. Core Privacy Principles to Follow

Global frameworks (GDPR, NIS2, EU Data Act, multiple U.S. state laws) and privacy‑by‑design guidance converge on a few core principles that individuals can adopt personally.[7][8][9][10]
