Paradigm
Systems Analysis

Naming the Problem, the Questions, and the Leverage — Through Meadows' Framework

Synthesized March 6, 2026
From Paradigm meeting notes, justNICE blog research,
Paradigm marketing analysis, current events research (Feb–Mar 2026),
and Donella Meadows' Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System

The Core Problem, Named

What Paradigm, justNICE, and the resilience research are circling around is a single problem with many faces:

People know something is deeply wrong but lack the language, the frameworks, and the pathways to act on what they know.

The crisis isn't just political. It's epistemic (how do I know what's true?), relational (who can I trust?), and operational (what do I actually do?). The justNICE blogs document the threats. The Paradigm meetings document the vehicle. But the gap between them — the "how do I" — is where most people are stuck.

Meadows' framework makes visible why this gap exists: most activism, most media, and most political debate operate at the lowest-leverage intervention points (adjusting parameters, arguing over numbers), while the system's actual dysfunction lives at the highest-leverage points (goals, paradigms, the capacity to self-organize). The "how do I" questions cluster at every level — but the ones people can't yet articulate are the ones at the top.

The Unanswered "How Do I…" Questions

These cluster into layers that map from the lowest to the highest leverage points in Meadows' framework:

Layer 1: Survival and Parameters (Leverage Points 12–11)

Constants, numbers, buffers — the level where most public discourse lives

  • How do I afford groceries when tariffs are raising prices and wages are stagnant?
  • How do I build an emergency fund when 60%+ of Americans live paycheck to paycheck?
  • How do I prepare without becoming a paranoid prepper — how do I hold readiness and hope at the same time?
  • How do I protect my family when the institutions supposed to do that are being dismantled?

Layer 2: Infrastructure and Delays (Leverage Points 10–9)

Physical/digital structure, time between action and consequence

  • How do I set up secure communications when every major platform is subject to subpoena?
  • How do I protect my data when AI chats have no legal privilege and OpenAI hands over 20 million logs?
  • How do I deal with the grief of watching this happen in slow motion — why does nothing seem to work fast enough?
  • How do I act when I don't know enough? The perfectionism trap: people wait for the full picture before acting, and the full picture never arrives.

Layer 3: Feedback and Power Loops (Leverage Points 8–7)

Accountability mechanisms, self-reinforcing cycles of concentration

  • How do I hold anyone accountable when courts issue orders the executive ignores?
  • How do I compete when the other side has unlimited money and a self-reinforcing wealth→power→wealth loop?
  • How do I run for office / support someone running when the system is built to crush outsiders?
  • Why does it feel like things keep accelerating? (Because positive feedback loops are running unchecked.)

Layer 4: Information, Rules, and Self-Organization (Leverage Points 6–4)

Who knows what, who writes the rules, the capacity to build new structures

  • How do I tell the difference between real threat and manufactured panic — when both are happening at once?
  • How do I find my people? Not allies in the abstract — actual humans in my neighborhood who will show up.
  • How do I organize when organizing itself is surveilled and targeted?
  • How do I build something that doesn't depend on institutions that are failing — without waiting for permission from those same institutions?
  • How do I contribute something real when I'm not a lawyer, not an activist, not a tech person — just someone who cares?

Layer 5: Goals, Paradigms, and Transcendence (Leverage Points 3–1)

Purpose, worldview, the capacity to hold multiple frameworks

  • What are we actually trying to build? What does winning look like?
  • How do I talk to people who don't see it — or who see it and have given up?
  • How do I move from doomscrolling to doing? (Learned helplessness is neurological, not moral. People aren't lazy — their brains are in shutdown.)
  • How do I hold hope and realism at the same time?
  • How do I act without certainty? How do I stay grounded when everything is shifting?

The Crisis

The current system's de facto goal is concentration and preservation of power. DOGE's stated goal is "efficiency"; actual goal: dismantling the administrative state. ICE's stated goal is "law enforcement"; actual goal: terror as policy (3,000 arrests/day directive, 73,000 in custody, 70%+ with no criminal record).

Your Work Here

Paradigm's deeper goal: redefining what a political operation optimizes for. Not "we help you win" but "professional campaigns shouldn't be reserved for the rich." justNICE's goal is agency restoration: giving people the information and tools to act on their own behalf. The word that ties it all together: agency. The crisis is fundamentally a crisis of agency.

2Paradigms (Worldviews)

The unstated assumptions that make the system's goals seem natural

The Crisis

The dominant paradigm: government is the problem, markets are the solution, individuals are responsible for their own outcomes, power is earned, the existing order is legitimate. The 5GW analysis names the mechanism by which paradigms are manufactured: cognitive warfare targets assumptions people don't know they hold.

This Is Where Your Writing Voice Matters Most

  • Surveillance reframed from "security" to "control"
  • Privacy reframed from "I have nothing to hide" to "your data is weaponized"
  • Preparedness reframed from "survivalist" to "community resilience"
  • Political consulting reframed from "expensive service for the powerful" to "infrastructure for the underdog"
  • Resilience reframed from "individual toughness" to "collective adaptive capacity"

Meadows: paradigm shifts happen when enough people see the old paradigm's failures clearly enough that a new one becomes thinkable.

1Transcending Paradigms

Recognizing all paradigms as tools, not truth — acting without certainty

The Crisis

Learned helplessness is neurological (ADAA research). The brain's anxiety-regulation mechanisms shut down under sustained, uncontrollable stress. The feeling underneath: the ground is moving, the institutions people assumed would hold are not holding. It's grief for a version of the country that at least provided a framework for believing things could get better.

Your Work Here

Operating across mutual aid, political infrastructure, systems theory, threat analysis, and privacy engineering — without collapsing them into a single ideology — is the practice of transcending paradigms. Can the "Campaign in a Box" include not just how-to guides but a way of thinking that lets people navigate without a map?

The Leverage Diagram Applied to Your Work

Leverage PointWhere Most Activism Gets StuckWhere Your Work Operates
12. Parameters"Raise the minimum wage"Paradigm's 10:1 wage parity (parameter that encodes a paradigm)
11. Buffers"Build an emergency fund"Emergency preparedness guide, layered redundancy
10. Stocks/Flows"Use Signal instead of SMS"Privacy/sovereignty infrastructure, secure tech stack
9. Delays"Vote them out in 2028"Campaign in a Box (shortens time from intention to action)
8. Negative Feedback"The courts will save us"KYR training, ICE tracking, electoral consulting
7. Positive Feedback"Money in politics is unstoppable"Contingency model for under-resourced candidates
6. Information Flows"Nobody knows what's really happening"justNICE blogs, ICE timelines, privacy guides
5. Rules"We need better laws"Paradigm: electing people who write the rules
4. Self-Organization"We need a leader"Resilience frameworks, mutual aid, community defense
3. Goals"We need to win elections"Anti-corruption mission, agency restoration
2. Paradigms"The system is broken"Reframing: individual survival → collective resilience
1. Transcendence"I need the right answer first"Multi-framework practice: act without certainty

The Key Insight

Most people and organizations operate at leverage points 12–9. They argue about numbers, build buffers, fix infrastructure, and complain about delays. The interventions that actually transform systems happen at 6–1: changing who has information, changing the rules, enabling self-organization, redefining goals, and shifting paradigms.

Your work is already concentrated in the 6–1 range.

Name the paradigm that's failing. Name the goal that should replace it. The rest follows.

The on-ramp — the missing piece that converts a reader into a participant — is the answer to: "How do I go from reading your blog post at 2am with my heart racing to actually being part of something?" The "Campaign in a Box" free resources are the closest thing to that on-ramp. The free strategy calls are the human bridge. But the language that names the problem for people — the words that make someone recognize their own experience — that's what converts awareness into agency.

Sources and Framework References

Meadows Framework:

  • Donella Meadows, "Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System" (1999) — donellameadows.org
  • Twelve Leverage Points — Wikipedia

Current Events (Feb–Mar 2026):

  • TIME, CBPP, CNN, NPR on DOGE layoffs and data access
  • HIAS, American Immigration Council, NEA on ICE enforcement and community impact
  • Carnegie Endowment, Protect Democracy, Democracy Docket on democratic backsliding
  • Stanford SIEPR, EPI, Bloomberg on economic conditions
  • ACLU, Bloomsbury Institute, Harvard Ash Center on surveillance
  • 50501 movement, Waging Nonviolence, Labor Network for Sustainability on resistance organizing
  • ADAA, Dame Magazine, APA on psychological impact

Internal Sources:

  • Paradigm meeting notes (Mar 6, 2026)
  • Paradigm agenda and campaign-in-a-box documents
  • Paradigm marketing analysis
  • justNICE blog posts: rebuilding resilience, 5GW research, AI chat legal risks, AI surveillance countermeasures, emergency preparedness, data privacy & sovereignty, anti-surveillance tech market, privacy protection (Merrill), ATX ICE activity, MN ICE activity

The PDF is at [Paradigm-systems-analysis.pdf](Resilience/Paradigm/Paradigm-systems-analysis.pdf) (89KB). It includes:

- Dark blue/red presentation-style cover page
- All six major sections with styled section headers and red accent borders
- The "Words to Describe the Problem" table with highlighted rows for high-leverage points (6–1)
- Leverage point cards with numbered badges (blue for standard, red for high-leverage)
- Callout boxes for key insights
- The full comparison diagram table
- Clean page breaks between major sections
- Page numbers in the footer

The only warning was the cover page `100vh` height — weasyprint doesn't support viewport units in print, so the cover page will be slightly shorter than full-page but still functions as a distinct title page. Let me know if you want any formatting adjustments.
